statement: Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson, leader of Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), delivered an opposition speech on 9 March 2026 accusing the government of conducting 'baktjaldamakk' (behind-the-scenes machinations) with the EU. He argued that the government's plans were first revealed through foreign media, not to Alþingi or the utanríkismálanefnd (foreign affairs committee). He claimed the summer referendum date was deliberately chosen to minimise public debate, and that the referendum question is misleading because EU accession negotiations require the applicant country to immediately begin adapting its laws and institutions — it is not simply 'looking at what's on offer'. He referenced the period 2009–2013 when EU representatives themselves tried to explain to Icelandic politicians that there is no such thing as exploratory negotiations without commitment to join. He characterised the government as having 'given up' on its domestic economic programme and using the EU referendum as a distraction.
source_name: Alþingi — Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson, þingmaður Miðflokksins (ræða, 157. löggjafarþing)
caveats: This is an opposition political speech. The characterisation of government actions as 'plotting' reflects Miðflokkurinn's strongly anti-EU stance. The claim that accession negotiations require immediate legal adaptation is broadly accurate for the acquis communautaire process but overstates the immediacy — adaptation timelines are negotiated chapter by chapter. The assertion that foreign media broke the story before domestic disclosure reflects a specific political narrative.

related_entries.statement: Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson, is aligned with the European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR) grouping and is strongly opposed to EU integration. In the context of the 2026 EU referendum campaign, Norwegian outlet Altinget.no reported that Miðflokkurinn representatives had met with US Republican Party contacts, and that a political advisor linked to Nigel Farage visited Iceland twice in early 2026. Heimildin also reported on these international connections. Sigmundur Davíð has cultivated ties with international conservative and Eurosceptic movements since founding the party in 2017 after splitting from the Progressive Party. The party holds 8 seats in Althingi as of session 157.

The coalition government formed in late 2024, comprising Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), agreed in their coalition agreement to hold a referendum on whether to resume EU accession negotiations by 2027. The referendum was subsequently scheduled for 29 August 2026. The question asks whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the EU — not whether to join. This is the first Icelandic government since Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (2009–2013) to actively pursue EU-related policy. Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) and Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) have been the primary government figures advancing the referendum. Previous governments, including those led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson (2013–2016) and Katrín Jakobsdóttir (2017–2024), did not pursue EU accession despite intermittent public calls for a referendum.

Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir presented a parliamentary resolution (stjórnartillaga til þingsályktunar) on 9 March 2026 proposing a referendum on 29 August 2026 with the question: 'Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?' (Should negotiations on Iceland's EU accession continue?). In her flutningsræða (sponsor's speech), she framed the referendum as a sovereignty issue — arguing that allowing the nation to decide is itself an exercise of sovereignty. She drew parallels with three historical decisions (NATO membership, EFTA accession, EEA agreement) that were also contentious at the time but are now widely regarded as beneficial. She stated that Iceland's 2009 EU application remains valid, confirmed by the EU itself, and that negotiations could resume 'around the turn of the year' if the public votes yes. She explicitly pledged: 'Ég mun aldrei skrifa undir samning sem tryggir ekki okkar markmið og okkar yfirráð' (I will never sign a deal that does not secure our goals and our sovereignty) — specifically regarding fisheries. She cited precedents for special arrangements: Nordic agricultural exemptions (Sweden, Finland) and property purchase restrictions (Denmark, Malta).

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Evrópusambandið reyndi ítrekað frá 2009 til 2013 að útskýra fyrir Íslandi að umsóknarferli um aðild snýst um að uppfylla kröfur ESB, ekki um samningaviðræður.
Ríkisstjórnin hefur verið í baktjaldamakki í Brussel frá 2024 um ESB-aðild.
Íslenska þjóðin frétti fyrst af ESB-áformum ríkisstjórnarinnar í erlendum fjölmiðlum sem höfðu upplýsingarnar frá heimildarmönnum í Brussel, ekki frá ríkisstjórninni sjálfri.
Ríkisstjórnin setti ESB-málið á oddinn vegna þess að hún var komin í þrot með efnahagsmálin sem hún var kosin út á — verðbólgu, vexti og stöðu heimilanna.
ESB-málið var ekki á þingmálaskrá ríkisstjórnarinnar og minnihlutinn hefur ítrekað þurft að fara fyrir fundarstjórn til að benda á brot á þingskapalögum.
